data.content_ref: inline:The claim asserts that hyper-specialization eliminated local resilience and that populations 'lacked the knowledge or tools to immediately return to autonomous subsistence,' yet the scope excludes 'Rural hinterlands'—precisely where generalized agrarian skills would have been concentrated and where the contrast between specialized and generalized populations would be tested. By excluding the hinterlands, the account avoids the observable implication that rural, less-specialized settlements should have survived better than palatial centers. If the hinterlands also collapsed, the mechanism fails; if they survived, their exclusion is an artificial restriction that shields the claim from falsification.
